{{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:TofukujiSanmon.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tōfuku-ji school|qid=Q126509804}}|prefecture={{ill|Higashiyama-ku|qid=Q1203862}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.975707|N|135.773676|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Enni|qid=Q739826}}; {{ill|Kujō Michiie|qid=Q324930}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qid=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Kyoto Gozan|qid=Q8193432}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2166743]] Wikidata name: Tōfuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{More footnotes needed|date=March 2010}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tōfuku-ji
東福寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = TofukujiHondo.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = | alt = | caption = [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 15-Chōme 778 Honmachi, [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyōto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|37.38|N|135|46|26.74|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Tōfuku-ji [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]], [[:en:Fuke Zen|Fuke]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head temple, [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain Temple (Kyoto)]] | website = http://www.tofukuji.jp/ | founded_by = [[:en:Enni|Enni Ben'en]] and [[:en:Kujō Michiie|Kujō Michiie]] |established=1236| year_completed = 1917 (Reconstruction) }} {{Fuke Zen}} {{nihongo|'''Tōfuku-ji'''|東福寺}} is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] in [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Tōfuku-ji takes its name from two temples in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]], [[Tōdai-ji]] and [[Kōfuku-ji]].Japan Reference: [http://www.wa-pedia.com/japan-guide/tofukuji_kyoto.shtml Tōfuku-ji] It is one of the [[:en:Five Mountain System|Kyoto ''Gozan'']] or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto". Its honorary ''[[Buddhist temples in Japan#sangō|sangō]]'' prefix is {{nihongo|'''Enichi-san'''|慧日山}}. ==History== Tōfuku-ji was founded in 1236 by the imperial [[:en:Chancellor|Chancellor]] [[:en:Kujō Michiie|Kujō Michiie]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869,'' p. 152. He appointed the monk [[:en:Enni|Enni]] as founding priest, who had studied [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai]] [[:en:Zen|Zen]] Buddhism in China under the monk [[:en:Wuzhun Shifan|Wuzhun Shifan]] and who founded [[:en:Jōten-ji|Jōten-ji]] temple in [[:en:Hakata|Hakata]] upon his return to his homeland. Tōfuku-ji temple burned down but was rebuilt in the 15th century according to original plans. It was because of this fire damage that a merchant ship was sent to Yuan China to replace damaged artifacts and to obtain special construction materials. The ship, however that later became known as the [[:en:Shinan ship|Shinan ship]] sank on her return journey close to Korean shores. Tōfuku-ji temple was regarded as a remarkable replica of the Chinese public monasteries in [[:en:Zhejiang Province|Zhejiang Province]], which Japanese monks frequently visited in the thirteenth century.{{Cite book |last1=Heine|last2=Wright |first1=Steven|first2=Dale S. |title=Zen Classics: Formative Texts in the History of Zen Buddhism |date=October 6, 2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0195175264|location=Oxford, United Kingdom |page=142| url= https://books.google.com/books?id=HAoZ-BELposC&dq=Kennin-ji+was+modeled+after+the+Chinese+Baizhang+Monastery+of+the+%5B%5B+%22Tang%22+Dynasty%5D%5D&pg=PA142}} Tofuku-ji was one of the five temples of the [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain System]]. The temple was greatly reduced in size from 70 buildings to 25 during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji]] era after the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree. In 1881, a fire burned down many major buildings such as the Main Hall, the ''Hōjo'', the ''Hattō'' and the statue of Sakyamuni Buddha. During the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], the temple area was requisitioned and became a [[:en:prisoner-of-war camp|prisoner-of-war camp]] for Russians. Both the main hall and the ''Hattō'' were rebuilt in 1917, and a new statue of Sakyamuni Buddha was later relocated to the temple in 1934. ==Abbots== In 1486 [[:en:Ryōan Keigo|Ryōan Keigo]] became the 171st abbot of Tōfuku-ji. At the end of the 16th century [[:en:Ankokuji Ekei|Ankokuji Ekei]] was appointed abbot. From 1980 to 2009 Tōfuku-ji has been led by head abbot [[:en:Keido Fukushima|Keidō Fukushima]].Harris, Ishwar C. ''et al.'' (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=oawiWve0GC0C ''The Laughing Buddha of Tofukuji: The Life of Zen Master Keidō Fukushima,'' p. xi.] ==Architecture== [[File:TofukujiSanmon.jpg|thumb|The main gate]] Tōfuku-ji's main gate is the oldest ''[[sanmon]]'' in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]] of Japan. It is two stories high and five bays wide. The central three bays are doors. Currently, the Tōfuku-ji complex includes 24 sub-temples, though in the past the number has been as high as 53. The complex includes Japan's oldest communal toilet, which was built in the first half of the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. In October 2022, a car crashed through and damaged the wooden doors and supporting pillars at the entrance to the toilet.{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-63304390 |publisher=[[:en:BBC News|BBC News]] |title=Japan heritage worker backs car into oldest toilet at Kyoto temple |date=18 October 2022 |access-date=18 October 2022}} ==Artwork== Tōfuku-ji's large ''nehan-zu'' painting depicts Buddha on his death bed. This massive image (7 x 14 meters) is the second largest in Japan. The image at nearby [[:en:Sennyū-ji|Sennyū-ji]] is the largest of its kind in Japan, measuring 8 x 16 meters. Both images are rarely displayed, most recently in 2003 for three days only.[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20030309k1.html "Kansai: Who -- What: Giant Buddhas shown for three days only,"] ''Japan Times Online.'' March 9, 2003. A 1238 [[:en:portrait painting|portrait painting]] of Wuzhun Shifan along with an inscription by an anonymous author was brought to the temple by Enni in the 1240s and remains there today. Plaques of Wuzhun's calligraphy are also kept at Tōfuku-ji. In 1933 the [[:en:Nihonga|Nihonga]] painter [[:en:Inshō Dōmoto|Inshō Dōmoto]] painted the large and vivid "Blue Dragon" ceiling painting for one of the halls, which he painted in 17 days.{{cite web |url=http://www.kyoto-magonote.jp/en/zen-2016/zen-2016.html |title=KYOTO MAGOnoTE｜zen-2016｜Special Spring Openings of the Zen Temples in Kyoto |website=www.kyoto-magonote.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160613164744/http://www.kyoto-magonote.jp/en/zen-2016/zen-2016.html |archive-date=2016-06-13}} ==Garden== [[File:Toufuku-ji hojyo7.JPG|thumb|right|The moss garden]] There are a number of [[:en:Japanese garden|gardens]] in the various precincts of Tōfuku-ji. The current gardens were designed by landscape architect [[:en:Mirei Shigemori|Mirei Shigemori]] in the summer and autumn of 1939. The moss garden in particular has been emblematic of the renewal of Japanese gardening principles in the 20th Century. The temple features a large number of [[:en:Acer palmatum|Japanese maple]] trees, and is most crowded during the autumn season when people flock to see the autumn foliage. It is a tradition to view the leaves from the Tsūten-kyō bridge. ==images== image:Toufuku-ji sanmon4.JPG|Main gate, side view image:FundainZunantei.jpg|View from interior image:Hiroshige People under maple trees by a stream.jpg|Scenic drawing of Tsūten-kyō bridge, by [[Hiroshige]] image:Tsuten-kyo bridge.JPG|Tsūten-kyō bridge during autumn image:Tofukuji Tsutenkyo.jpg|Tsūten-kyō image:ReiunIn GaunNoNiwa.jpg|Dry Zen garden at Reiun-in image:Toufuku-ji kaizandou3.JPG|Garden at Kaizandō image:TofukujiRyoginanToutei.jpg|Garden at Ryoginan-tōtei image:Tofukuji torii.jpg|''Torii'' gates leading to steps image:Tofukuji stairs.jpg|Steps image:Toufuku-ji hondou2.JPG|Main hall image:TofukujiKaisando.jpg|[[:en:Kaisando|Kaizandō]] image:Tofukuji-bridge-autumn-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|Maple trees in [[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] around Tsūten-kyō image:Tofukuji-maple-autumn-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of maple trees from Tsūten-kyō ==Shinan Ship== [[File:Wooden labels from Shinan Ship with Tofuku-ji written on them.jpg|thumb|Wooden Tags(木間) from the Shinan Shipwreck, ALL with Tofuku-ji(東福寺) Inscriptions]] As mentioned in the “History” section of this article, Tōfuku-ji temple was severely damaged and partly destroyed by fires during the second and third decades of the 14th century. It was specifically in response to a fire damage in 1319 that a merchant ship was sent to Yuan China in 1323 to replace damaged artifacts and to obtain special construction materials for the reconstruction. The ship that now is known as [[:en:Shinan ship|Shinan ship]], sailed from [[:en:Ningbo|Ningbo]] to Hakata. The early 14th century journey was never completed though as the ship with its 200 tons of cargo was caught in a storm and sank close to Korean shores. This became apparent only after the wreckage was found almost seven hundred years later in 1975 close to the Shinan Islands. On many of the “wooden tablets” or “wooden tags”(木間) that were used customarily to identify the cargo, the Chinese calligraphy characters of Tōfuku-ji(東福寺) temple could be clearly read. Miraculously her passengers, some (or many) of them Japanese Buddhist monks, as recorded by the Korean annals [[:en:Goryeosa|Goryeosa]], drifted ashore sound and safe. We can also find records of this unfavorable but overall lucky incident in the personal notes of zen monk-poet [[:en:Daichi Sokei|Daichi Zenji]](大智禅師) who happened to be one of the repatriated passengers.{{Cite web|date=2019-07-05|title=The Return of the Celadons|url=https://gwangjunewsgic.com/arts-culture/exhibitions/the-return-of-the-celadons/|access-date=2024-03-09|website=[[Gwangju News]]}}{{Cite web|date=2021-04-24|title=新案船 (Shinan Ship) Folder|url=https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=Iw87eZhyO0A&t=2s&pp=ygURU2hpbmFuIHNoaXAgZm9sZGU%3D|access-date=2024-03-09|website= titled video, please check at 2 min 02 sec. to see “Tofuku-ji” characters on one of the wooden labels(from [[youtube]] channel of user “chang gyong”)}} ==See also== *[[:en:Zen|Zen]] *[[:en:Enni|Enni]] *[[:en:Wuzhun Shifan|Wuzhun Shifan]] *[[:en:List of Buddhist temples|List of Buddhist temples]] *[[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC ''The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism.''] New York: Rosen Publishing Group. {{ISBN|978-0-8239-2240-6}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/42680558 OCLC 42680558] * [[:en:Heinrich Dumoulin|Dumoulin]], Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: [[:en:World Wisdom|World Wisdom]]. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * [[:en:Keido Fukushima|Fukushima]], Keidō and Fumi Dan. (2006). ''Tōfukuji.'' Kyoto: Tankōsha. {{ISBN|978-4-473-03353-6}}; {{OCLC|145752382}} * Harris, Ishwar C. and Jeff Shore. (2004). [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/56051074 ''The Laughing Buddha of Tofukuji: The Life of Zen Master Keidō Fukushima.''] Bloomington, Indiana: World Wisdom. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-62-4}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/56051074 OCLC 56051074] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |type= Hardback |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * [http://www.tofukuji.jp Tōfuku-ji official web site] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/tofukuji/ Tōfuku-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/05tofuku.html Tōfuku-ji] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?id=120,318,0,0,1,0 Tōfuku-ji] * [http://damien.douxchamps.net/photo/japan/kansai/kyoto/tofukuji Pictures of Tōfuku-ji] {{Commons Category|Tofukuji|Tōfuku-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1230s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1236 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Rinzai temples]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Zen gardens]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] }} [[en:Tōfuku-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Qq 4]] [[ar:توفوكو-جي]] [[arz:توفوكو جى]] [[ca:Tōfuku-ji]] {{Commons category|Tofukuji}} [[de:Tōfuku-ji]] [[el:Τοφούκου-τζι]] [[fa:توفوکو-جی]] [[fr:Tōfuku-ji]] [[gan:東福寺]] [[id:Tōfuku-ji]] [[it:Tōfuku-ji]] [[ja:東福寺]] [[ko:도후쿠지]] [[pl:Tōfuku-ji]] [[pnb:ٹوفوکو-جی]] [[ru:Тофуку-дзи]] [[tr:Tōfuku-ji]] [[uk:Тофуку-дзі]] [[wuu:东福寺]] [[zh:東福寺]] [[zh-classical:東福寺]] {{wikidata link|Q2166743}}

東福寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = TofukujiHondo.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = | alt = | caption = [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 15-Chōme 778 Honmachi, [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyōto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|37.38|N|135|46|26.74|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Tōfuku-ji [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]], [[:en:Fuke Zen|Fuke]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head temple, [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain Temple (Kyoto)]] | website = http://www.tofukuji.jp/ | founded_by = [[:en:Enni|Enni Ben'en]] and [[:en:Kujō Michiie|Kujō Michiie]] |established=1236| year_completed = 1917 (Reconstruction) }} {{Fuke Zen}} {{nihongo|'''Tōfuku-ji'''|東福寺}} is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] in [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Tōfuku-ji takes its name from two temples in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]], [[Tōdai-ji]] and [[Kōfuku-ji]].Japan Reference: [http://www.wa-pedia.com/japan-guide/tofukuji_kyoto.shtml Tōfuku-ji] It is one of the [[:en:Five Mountain System|Kyoto ''Gozan'']] or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto". Its honorary ''[[Buddhist temples in Japan#sangō|sangō]]'' prefix is {{nihongo|'''Enichi-san'''|慧日山}}. ==History== Tōfuku-ji was founded in 1236 by the imperial [[:en:Chancellor|Chancellor]] [[:en:Kujō Michiie|Kujō Michiie]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869,'' p. 152. He appointed the monk [[:en:Enni|Enni]] as founding priest, who had studied [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai]] [[:en:Zen|Zen]] Buddhism in China under the monk [[:en:Wuzhun Shifan|Wuzhun Shifan]] and who founded [[:en:Jōten-ji|Jōten-ji]] temple in [[:en:Hakata|Hakata]] upon his return to his homeland. Tōfuku-ji temple burned down but was rebuilt in the 15th century according to original plans. It was because of this fire damage that a merchant ship was sent to Yuan China to replace damaged artifacts and to obtain special construction materials. The ship, however that later became known as the [[:en:Shinan ship|Shinan ship]] sank on her return journey close to Korean shores. Tōfuku-ji temple was regarded as a remarkable replica of the Chinese public monasteries in [[:en:Zhejiang Province|Zhejiang Province]], which Japanese monks frequently visited in the thirteenth century.{{Cite book |last1=Heine|last2=Wright |first1=Steven|first2=Dale S. |title=Zen Classics: Formative Texts in the History of Zen Buddhism |date=October 6, 2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0195175264|location=Oxford, United Kingdom |page=142| url= https://books.google.com/books?id=HAoZ-BELposC&dq=Kennin-ji+was+modeled+after+the+Chinese+Baizhang+Monastery+of+the+%5B%5B+%22Tang%22+Dynasty%5D%5D&pg=PA142}} Tofuku-ji was one of the five temples of the [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain System]]. The temple was greatly reduced in size from 70 buildings to 25 during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji]] era after the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree. In 1881, a fire burned down many major buildings such as the Main Hall, the ''Hōjo'', the ''Hattō'' and the statue of Sakyamuni Buddha. During the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], the temple area was requisitioned and became a [[:en:prisoner-of-war camp|prisoner-of-war camp]] for Russians. Both the main hall and the ''Hattō'' were rebuilt in 1917, and a new statue of Sakyamuni Buddha was later relocated to the temple in 1934. ==Abbots== In 1486 [[:en:Ryōan Keigo|Ryōan Keigo]] became the 171st abbot of Tōfuku-ji. At the end of the 16th century [[:en:Ankokuji Ekei|Ankokuji Ekei]] was appointed abbot. From 1980 to 2009 Tōfuku-ji has been led by head abbot [[:en:Keido Fukushima|Keidō Fukushima]].Harris, Ishwar C. ''et al.'' (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=oawiWve0GC0C ''The Laughing Buddha of Tofukuji: The Life of Zen Master Keidō Fukushima,'' p. xi.] ==Architecture== [[File:TofukujiSanmon.jpg|thumb|The main gate]] Tōfuku-ji's main gate is the oldest ''[[sanmon]]'' in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]] of Japan. It is two stories high and five bays wide. The central three bays are doors. Currently, the Tōfuku-ji complex includes 24 sub-temples, though in the past the number has been as high as 53. The complex includes Japan's oldest communal toilet, which was built in the first half of the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. In October 2022, a car crashed through and damaged the wooden doors and supporting pillars at the entrance to the toilet.{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-63304390 |publisher=[[:en:BBC News|BBC News]] |title=Japan heritage worker backs car into oldest toilet at Kyoto temple |date=18 October 2022 |access-date=18 October 2022}} ==Artwork== Tōfuku-ji's large ''nehan-zu'' painting depicts Buddha on his death bed. This massive image (7 x 14 meters) is the second largest in Japan. The image at nearby [[:en:Sennyū-ji|Sennyū-ji]] is the largest of its kind in Japan, measuring 8 x 16 meters. Both images are rarely displayed, most recently in 2003 for three days only.[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20030309k1.html "Kansai: Who -- What: Giant Buddhas shown for three days only,"] ''Japan Times Online.'' March 9, 2003. A 1238 [[:en:portrait painting|portrait painting]] of Wuzhun Shifan along with an inscription by an anonymous author was brought to the temple by Enni in the 1240s and remains there today. Plaques of Wuzhun's calligraphy are also kept at Tōfuku-ji. In 1933 the [[:en:Nihonga|Nihonga]] painter [[:en:Inshō Dōmoto|Inshō Dōmoto]] painted the large and vivid "Blue Dragon" ceiling painting for one of the halls, which he painted in 17 days.{{cite web |url=http://www.kyoto-magonote.jp/en/zen-2016/zen-2016.html |title=KYOTO MAGOnoTE｜zen-2016｜Special Spring Openings of the Zen Temples in Kyoto |website=www.kyoto-magonote.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160613164744/http://www.kyoto-magonote.jp/en/zen-2016/zen-2016.html |archive-date=2016-06-13}} ==Garden== [[File:Toufuku-ji hojyo7.JPG|thumb|right|The moss garden]] There are a number of [[:en:Japanese garden|gardens]] in the various precincts of Tōfuku-ji. The current gardens were designed by landscape architect [[:en:Mirei Shigemori|Mirei Shigemori]] in the summer and autumn of 1939. The moss garden in particular has been emblematic of the renewal of Japanese gardening principles in the 20th Century. The temple features a large number of [[:en:Acer palmatum|Japanese maple]] trees, and is most crowded during the autumn season when people flock to see the autumn foliage. It is a tradition to view the leaves from the Tsūten-kyō bridge. ==images== image:Toufuku-ji sanmon4.JPG|Main gate, side view image:FundainZunantei.jpg|View from interior image:Hiroshige People under maple trees by a stream.jpg|Scenic drawing of Tsūten-kyō bridge, by [[Hiroshige]] image:Tsuten-kyo bridge.JPG|Tsūten-kyō bridge during autumn image:Tofukuji Tsutenkyo.jpg|Tsūten-kyō image:ReiunIn GaunNoNiwa.jpg|Dry Zen garden at Reiun-in image:Toufuku-ji kaizandou3.JPG|Garden at Kaizandō image:TofukujiRyoginanToutei.jpg|Garden at Ryoginan-tōtei image:Tofukuji torii.jpg|''Torii'' gates leading to steps image:Tofukuji stairs.jpg|Steps image:Toufuku-ji hondou2.JPG|Main hall image:TofukujiKaisando.jpg|[[:en:Kaisando|Kaizandō]] image:Tofukuji-bridge-autumn-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|Maple trees in [[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] around Tsūten-kyō image:Tofukuji-maple-autumn-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of maple trees from Tsūten-kyō ==Shinan Ship== [[File:Wooden labels from Shinan Ship with Tofuku-ji written on them.jpg|thumb|Wooden Tags(木間) from the Shinan Shipwreck, ALL with Tofuku-ji(東福寺) Inscriptions]] As mentioned in the “History” section of this article, Tōfuku-ji temple was severely damaged and partly destroyed by fires during the second and third decades of the 14th century. It was specifically in response to a fire damage in 1319 that a merchant ship was sent to Yuan China in 1323 to replace damaged artifacts and to obtain special construction materials for the reconstruction. The ship that now is known as [[:en:Shinan ship|Shinan ship]], sailed from [[:en:Ningbo|Ningbo]] to Hakata. The early 14th century journey was never completed though as the ship with its 200 tons of cargo was caught in a storm and sank close to Korean shores. This became apparent only after the wreckage was found almost seven hundred years later in 1975 close to the Shinan Islands. On many of the “wooden tablets” or “wooden tags”(木間) that were used customarily to identify the cargo, the Chinese calligraphy characters of Tōfuku-ji(東福寺) temple could be clearly read. Miraculously her passengers, some (or many) of them Japanese Buddhist monks, as recorded by the Korean annals [[:en:Goryeosa|Goryeosa]], drifted ashore sound and safe. We can also find records of this unfavorable but overall lucky incident in the personal notes of zen monk-poet [[:en:Daichi Sokei|Daichi Zenji]](大智禅師) who happened to be one of the repatriated passengers.{{Cite web|date=2019-07-05|title=The Return of the Celadons|url=https://gwangjunewsgic.com/arts-culture/exhibitions/the-return-of-the-celadons/|access-date=2024-03-09|website=[[Gwangju News]]}}{{Cite web|date=2021-04-24|title=新案船 (Shinan Ship) Folder|url=https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=Iw87eZhyO0A&t=2s&pp=ygURU2hpbmFuIHNoaXAgZm9sZGU%3D|access-date=2024-03-09|website= titled video, please check at 2 min 02 sec. to see “Tofuku-ji” characters on one of the wooden labels(from [[youtube]] channel of user “chang gyong”)}} ==See also== *[[:en:Zen|Zen]] *[[:en:Enni|Enni]] *[[:en:Wuzhun Shifan|Wuzhun Shifan]] *[[:en:List of Buddhist temples|List of Buddhist temples]] *[[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC ''The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism.''] New York: Rosen Publishing Group. {{ISBN|978-0-8239-2240-6}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/42680558 OCLC 42680558] * [[:en:Heinrich Dumoulin|Dumoulin]], Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: [[:en:World Wisdom|World Wisdom]]. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * [[:en:Keido Fukushima|Fukushima]], Keidō and Fumi Dan. (2006). ''Tōfukuji.'' Kyoto: Tankōsha. {{ISBN|978-4-473-03353-6}}; {{OCLC|145752382}} * Harris, Ishwar C. and Jeff Shore. (2004). [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/56051074 ''The Laughing Buddha of Tofukuji: The Life of Zen Master Keidō Fukushima.''] Bloomington, Indiana: World Wisdom. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-62-4}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/56051074 OCLC 56051074] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |type= Hardback |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * [http://www.tofukuji.jp Tōfuku-ji official web site] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/tofukuji/ Tōfuku-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/05tofuku.html Tōfuku-ji] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?id=120,318,0,0,1,0 Tōfuku-ji] * [http://damien.douxchamps.net/photo/japan/kansai/kyoto/tofukuji Pictures of Tōfuku-ji] {{Commons Category|Tofukuji|Tōfuku-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1230s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1236 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Rinzai temples]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Zen gardens]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] }} [[en:Tōfuku-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ar:توفوكو-جي]] [[arz:توفوكو جى]] [[ca:Tōfuku-ji]] {{Commons category|Tofukuji}} [[de:Tōfuku-ji]] [[el:Τοφούκου-τζι]] [[fa:توفوکو-جی]] [[fr:Tōfuku-ji]] [[gan:東福寺]] [[id:Tōfuku-ji]] [[it:Tōfuku-ji]] [[ja:東福寺]] [[ko:도후쿠지]] [[pl:Tōfuku-ji]] [[pnb:ٹوفوکو-جی]] [[ru:Тофуку-дзи]] [[tr:Tōfuku-ji]] [[uk:Тофуку-дзі]] [[wuu:东福寺]] [[zh:東福寺]] [[zh-classical:東福寺]] {{wikidata link|Q2166743}}

[[ar:توفوكو-جي]][[arz:توفوكو جى]][[ca:Tōfuku-ji]][[commons:Category:Tofukuji]][[de:Tōfuku-ji]][[el:Τοφούκου-τζι]][[en:Tōfuku-ji]][[fa:توفوکو-جی]][[fr:Tōfuku-ji]][[gan:東福寺]][[id:Tōfuku-ji]][[it:Tōfuku-ji]][[ja:東福寺]][[ko:도후쿠지]][[pl:Tōfuku-ji]][[pnb:ٹوفوکو-جی]][[ru:Тофуку-дзи]][[tr:Tōfuku-ji]][[uk:Тофуку-дзі]][[wuu:东福寺]][[zh_classical:東福寺]][[zh:東福寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:TofukujiSanmon.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tōfuku-ji school|qid=Q126509804}}|prefecture={{ill|Higashiyama-ku|qid=Q1203862}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.975707|N|135.773676|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Enni|qid=Q739826}}; {{ill|Kujō Michiie|qid=Q324930}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qid=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Kyoto Gozan|qid=Q8193432}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2166743]] Wikidata name: Tōfuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{More footnotes needed|date=March 2010}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tōfuku-ji
東福寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = TofukujiHondo.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = | alt = | caption = [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 15-Chōme 778 Honmachi, [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyōto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|37.38|N|135|46|26.74|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Tōfuku-ji [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]], [[:en:Fuke Zen|Fuke]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head temple, [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain Temple (Kyoto)]] | website = http://www.tofukuji.jp/ | founded_by = [[:en:Enni|Enni Ben'en]] and [[:en:Kujō Michiie|Kujō Michiie]] |established=1236| year_completed = 1917 (Reconstruction) }} {{Fuke Zen}} {{nihongo|'''Tōfuku-ji'''|東福寺}} is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] in [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Tōfuku-ji takes its name from two temples in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]], [[Tōdai-ji]] and [[Kōfuku-ji]].Japan Reference: [http://www.wa-pedia.com/japan-guide/tofukuji_kyoto.shtml Tōfuku-ji] It is one of the [[:en:Five Mountain System|Kyoto ''Gozan'']] or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto". Its honorary ''[[Buddhist temples in Japan#sangō|sangō]]'' prefix is {{nihongo|'''Enichi-san'''|慧日山}}. ==History== Tōfuku-ji was founded in 1236 by the imperial [[:en:Chancellor|Chancellor]] [[:en:Kujō Michiie|Kujō Michiie]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869,'' p. 152. He appointed the monk [[:en:Enni|Enni]] as founding priest, who had studied [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai]] [[:en:Zen|Zen]] Buddhism in China under the monk [[:en:Wuzhun Shifan|Wuzhun Shifan]] and who founded [[:en:Jōten-ji|Jōten-ji]] temple in [[:en:Hakata|Hakata]] upon his return to his homeland. Tōfuku-ji temple burned down but was rebuilt in the 15th century according to original plans. It was because of this fire damage that a merchant ship was sent to Yuan China to replace damaged artifacts and to obtain special construction materials. The ship, however that later became known as the [[:en:Shinan ship|Shinan ship]] sank on her return journey close to Korean shores. Tōfuku-ji temple was regarded as a remarkable replica of the Chinese public monasteries in [[:en:Zhejiang Province|Zhejiang Province]], which Japanese monks frequently visited in the thirteenth century.{{Cite book |last1=Heine|last2=Wright |first1=Steven|first2=Dale S. |title=Zen Classics: Formative Texts in the History of Zen Buddhism |date=October 6, 2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0195175264|location=Oxford, United Kingdom |page=142| url= https://books.google.com/books?id=HAoZ-BELposC&dq=Kennin-ji+was+modeled+after+the+Chinese+Baizhang+Monastery+of+the+%5B%5B+%22Tang%22+Dynasty%5D%5D&pg=PA142}} Tofuku-ji was one of the five temples of the [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain System]]. The temple was greatly reduced in size from 70 buildings to 25 during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji]] era after the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree. In 1881, a fire burned down many major buildings such as the Main Hall, the ''Hōjo'', the ''Hattō'' and the statue of Sakyamuni Buddha. During the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], the temple area was requisitioned and became a [[:en:prisoner-of-war camp|prisoner-of-war camp]] for Russians. Both the main hall and the ''Hattō'' were rebuilt in 1917, and a new statue of Sakyamuni Buddha was later relocated to the temple in 1934. ==Abbots== In 1486 [[:en:Ryōan Keigo|Ryōan Keigo]] became the 171st abbot of Tōfuku-ji. At the end of the 16th century [[:en:Ankokuji Ekei|Ankokuji Ekei]] was appointed abbot. From 1980 to 2009 Tōfuku-ji has been led by head abbot [[:en:Keido Fukushima|Keidō Fukushima]].Harris, Ishwar C. ''et al.'' (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=oawiWve0GC0C ''The Laughing Buddha of Tofukuji: The Life of Zen Master Keidō Fukushima,'' p. xi.] ==Architecture== [[File:TofukujiSanmon.jpg|thumb|The main gate]] Tōfuku-ji's main gate is the oldest ''[[sanmon]]'' in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]] of Japan. It is two stories high and five bays wide. The central three bays are doors. Currently, the Tōfuku-ji complex includes 24 sub-temples, though in the past the number has been as high as 53. The complex includes Japan's oldest communal toilet, which was built in the first half of the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. In October 2022, a car crashed through and damaged the wooden doors and supporting pillars at the entrance to the toilet.{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-63304390 |publisher=[[:en:BBC News|BBC News]] |title=Japan heritage worker backs car into oldest toilet at Kyoto temple |date=18 October 2022 |access-date=18 October 2022}} ==Artwork== Tōfuku-ji's large ''nehan-zu'' painting depicts Buddha on his death bed. This massive image (7 x 14 meters) is the second largest in Japan. The image at nearby [[:en:Sennyū-ji|Sennyū-ji]] is the largest of its kind in Japan, measuring 8 x 16 meters. Both images are rarely displayed, most recently in 2003 for three days only.[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20030309k1.html "Kansai: Who -- What: Giant Buddhas shown for three days only,"] ''Japan Times Online.'' March 9, 2003. A 1238 [[:en:portrait painting|portrait painting]] of Wuzhun Shifan along with an inscription by an anonymous author was brought to the temple by Enni in the 1240s and remains there today. Plaques of Wuzhun's calligraphy are also kept at Tōfuku-ji. In 1933 the [[:en:Nihonga|Nihonga]] painter [[:en:Inshō Dōmoto|Inshō Dōmoto]] painted the large and vivid "Blue Dragon" ceiling painting for one of the halls, which he painted in 17 days.{{cite web |url=http://www.kyoto-magonote.jp/en/zen-2016/zen-2016.html |title=KYOTO MAGOnoTE｜zen-2016｜Special Spring Openings of the Zen Temples in Kyoto |website=www.kyoto-magonote.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160613164744/http://www.kyoto-magonote.jp/en/zen-2016/zen-2016.html |archive-date=2016-06-13}} ==Garden== [[File:Toufuku-ji hojyo7.JPG|thumb|right|The moss garden]] There are a number of [[:en:Japanese garden|gardens]] in the various precincts of Tōfuku-ji. The current gardens were designed by landscape architect [[:en:Mirei Shigemori|Mirei Shigemori]] in the summer and autumn of 1939. The moss garden in particular has been emblematic of the renewal of Japanese gardening principles in the 20th Century. The temple features a large number of [[:en:Acer palmatum|Japanese maple]] trees, and is most crowded during the autumn season when people flock to see the autumn foliage. It is a tradition to view the leaves from the Tsūten-kyō bridge. ==images== image:Toufuku-ji sanmon4.JPG|Main gate, side view image:FundainZunantei.jpg|View from interior image:Hiroshige People under maple trees by a stream.jpg|Scenic drawing of Tsūten-kyō bridge, by [[Hiroshige]] image:Tsuten-kyo bridge.JPG|Tsūten-kyō bridge during autumn image:Tofukuji Tsutenkyo.jpg|Tsūten-kyō image:ReiunIn GaunNoNiwa.jpg|Dry Zen garden at Reiun-in image:Toufuku-ji kaizandou3.JPG|Garden at Kaizandō image:TofukujiRyoginanToutei.jpg|Garden at Ryoginan-tōtei image:Tofukuji torii.jpg|''Torii'' gates leading to steps image:Tofukuji stairs.jpg|Steps image:Toufuku-ji hondou2.JPG|Main hall image:TofukujiKaisando.jpg|[[:en:Kaisando|Kaizandō]] image:Tofukuji-bridge-autumn-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|Maple trees in [[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] around Tsūten-kyō image:Tofukuji-maple-autumn-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of maple trees from Tsūten-kyō ==Shinan Ship== [[File:Wooden labels from Shinan Ship with Tofuku-ji written on them.jpg|thumb|Wooden Tags(木間) from the Shinan Shipwreck, ALL with Tofuku-ji(東福寺) Inscriptions]] As mentioned in the “History” section of this article, Tōfuku-ji temple was severely damaged and partly destroyed by fires during the second and third decades of the 14th century. It was specifically in response to a fire damage in 1319 that a merchant ship was sent to Yuan China in 1323 to replace damaged artifacts and to obtain special construction materials for the reconstruction. The ship that now is known as [[:en:Shinan ship|Shinan ship]], sailed from [[:en:Ningbo|Ningbo]] to Hakata. The early 14th century journey was never completed though as the ship with its 200 tons of cargo was caught in a storm and sank close to Korean shores. This became apparent only after the wreckage was found almost seven hundred years later in 1975 close to the Shinan Islands. On many of the “wooden tablets” or “wooden tags”(木間) that were used customarily to identify the cargo, the Chinese calligraphy characters of Tōfuku-ji(東福寺) temple could be clearly read. Miraculously her passengers, some (or many) of them Japanese Buddhist monks, as recorded by the Korean annals [[:en:Goryeosa|Goryeosa]], drifted ashore sound and safe. We can also find records of this unfavorable but overall lucky incident in the personal notes of zen monk-poet [[:en:Daichi Sokei|Daichi Zenji]](大智禅師) who happened to be one of the repatriated passengers.{{Cite web|date=2019-07-05|title=The Return of the Celadons|url=https://gwangjunewsgic.com/arts-culture/exhibitions/the-return-of-the-celadons/|access-date=2024-03-09|website=[[Gwangju News]]}}{{Cite web|date=2021-04-24|title=新案船 (Shinan Ship) Folder|url=https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=Iw87eZhyO0A&t=2s&pp=ygURU2hpbmFuIHNoaXAgZm9sZGU%3D|access-date=2024-03-09|website= titled video, please check at 2 min 02 sec. to see “Tofuku-ji” characters on one of the wooden labels(from [[youtube]] channel of user “chang gyong”)}} ==See also== *[[:en:Zen|Zen]] *[[:en:Enni|Enni]] *[[:en:Wuzhun Shifan|Wuzhun Shifan]] *[[:en:List of Buddhist temples|List of Buddhist temples]] *[[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC ''The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism.''] New York: Rosen Publishing Group. {{ISBN|978-0-8239-2240-6}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/42680558 OCLC 42680558] * [[:en:Heinrich Dumoulin|Dumoulin]], Heinrich. [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: [[:en:World Wisdom|World Wisdom]]. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * [[:en:Keido Fukushima|Fukushima]], Keidō and Fumi Dan. (2006). ''Tōfukuji.'' Kyoto: Tankōsha. {{ISBN|978-4-473-03353-6}}; {{OCLC|145752382}} * Harris, Ishwar C. and Jeff Shore. (2004). [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/56051074 ''The Laughing Buddha of Tofukuji: The Life of Zen Master Keidō Fukushima.''] Bloomington, Indiana: World Wisdom. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-62-4}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/56051074 OCLC 56051074] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |type= Hardback |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * [http://www.tofukuji.jp Tōfuku-ji official web site] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/tofukuji/ Tōfuku-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/05tofuku.html Tōfuku-ji] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?id=120,318,0,0,1,0 Tōfuku-ji] * [http://damien.douxchamps.net/photo/japan/kansai/kyoto/tofukuji Pictures of Tōfuku-ji] {{Commons Category|Tofukuji|Tōfuku-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1230s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1236 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Rinzai temples]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Zen gardens]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] }} [[en:Tōfuku-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ar:توفوكو-جي]] [[arz:توفوكو جى]] [[ca:Tōfuku-ji]] {{Commons category|Tofukuji}} [[de:Tōfuku-ji]] [[el:Τοφούκου-τζι]] [[fa:توفوکو-جی]] [[fr:Tōfuku-ji]] [[gan:東福寺]] [[id:Tōfuku-ji]] [[it:Tōfuku-ji]] [[ja:東福寺]] [[ko:도후쿠지]] [[pl:Tōfuku-ji]] [[pnb:ٹوفوکو-جی]] [[ru:Тофуку-дзи]] [[tr:Tōfuku-ji]] [[uk:Тофуку-дзі]] [[wuu:东福寺]] [[zh:東福寺]] [[zh-classical:東福寺]] {{wikidata link|Q2166743}}

revision.text:
[[ar:توفوكو-جي]][[arz:توفوكو جى]][[ca:Tōfuku-ji]][[sn:commons:Category:Tofukuji]][[de:Tōfuku-ji]][[el:Τοφούκου-τζι]][[en:Tōfuku-ji]][[fa:توفوکو-جی]][[fr:Tōfuku-ji]][[gan:東福寺]][[id:Tōfuku-ji]][[it:Tōfuku-ji]][[ja:東福寺]][[ko:도후쿠지]][[pl:Tōfuku-ji]][[pnb:ٹوفوکو-جی]][[ru:Тофуку-дзи]][[tr:Tōfuku-ji]][[uk:Тофуку-дзі]][[wuu:东福寺]][[zh_classical:東福寺]][[zh:東福寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:TofukujiSanmon.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tōfuku-ji school|qid=Q126509804}}|prefecture={{ill|Higashiyama-ku|qid=Q1203862}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.975707|N|135.773676|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Enni|qid=Q739826}}; {{ill|Kujō Michiie|qid=Q324930}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|qid=Q11644537}}|part_of={{ill|Kyoto Gozan|qid=Q8193432}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q2166743]] Wikidata name: Tōfuku-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:12, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{More footnotes needed|date=March 2010}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Tōfuku-ji
東福寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = TofukujiHondo.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = | alt = | caption = [[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 15-Chōme 778 Honmachi, [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyōto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|37.38|N|135|46|26.74|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Tōfuku-ji [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai school]], [[:en:Fuke Zen|Fuke]] | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head temple, [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain Temple (Kyoto)]] | website = http://www.tofukuji.jp/ | founded_by = [[:en:Enni|Enni Ben'en]] and [[:en:Kujō Michiie|Kujō Michiie]] |established=1236| year_completed = 1917 (Reconstruction) }} {{Fuke Zen}} {{nihongo|'''Tōfuku-ji'''|東福寺}} is a [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] [[:en:Temple|Temple]] in [[:en:Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|Higashiyama-ku]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. Tōfuku-ji takes its name from two temples in [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]], [[Tōdai-ji]] and [[Kōfuku-ji]].Japan Reference: [http://www.wa-pedia.com/japan-guide/tofukuji_kyoto.shtml Tōfuku-ji] It is one of the [[:en:Five Mountain System|Kyoto ''Gozan'']] or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto". Its honorary ''[[Buddhist temples in Japan#sangō|sangō]]'' prefix is {{nihongo|'''Enichi-san'''|慧日山}}. ==History== Tōfuku-ji was founded in 1236 by the imperial [[:en:Chancellor|Chancellor]] [[:en:Kujō Michiie|Kujō Michiie]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869,'' p. 152. He appointed the monk [[:en:Enni|Enni]] as founding priest, who had studied [[:en:Rinzai school|Rinzai]] [[:en:Zen|Zen]] Buddhism in China under the monk [[:en:Wuzhun Shifan|Wuzhun Shifan]] and who founded [[:en:Jōten-ji|Jōten-ji]] temple in [[:en:Hakata|Hakata]] upon his return to his homeland. Tōfuku-ji temple burned down but was rebuilt in the 15th century according to original plans. It was because of this fire damage that a merchant ship was sent to Yuan China to replace damaged artifacts and to obtain special construction materials. The ship, however that later became known as the [[:en:Shinan ship|Shinan ship]] sank on her return journey close to Korean shores. Tōfuku-ji temple was regarded as a remarkable replica of the Chinese public monasteries in [[:en:Zhejiang Province|Zhejiang Province]], which Japanese monks frequently visited in the thirteenth century.{{Cite book |last1=Heine|last2=Wright |first1=Steven|first2=Dale S. |title=Zen Classics: Formative Texts in the History of Zen Buddhism |date=October 6, 2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0195175264|location=Oxford, United Kingdom |page=142| url= https://books.google.com/books?id=HAoZ-BELposC&dq=Kennin-ji+was+modeled+after+the+Chinese+Baizhang+Monastery+of+the+%5B%5B+%22Tang%22+Dynasty%5D%5D&pg=PA142}} Tofuku-ji was one of the five temples of the [[:en:Five Mountain System|Five Mountain System]]. The temple was greatly reduced in size from 70 buildings to 25 during the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji]] era after the [[Shinbutsu bunri]] decree. In 1881, a fire burned down many major buildings such as the Main Hall, the ''Hōjo'', the ''Hattō'' and the statue of Sakyamuni Buddha. During the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], the temple area was requisitioned and became a [[:en:prisoner-of-war camp|prisoner-of-war camp]] for Russians. Both the main hall and the ''Hattō'' were rebuilt in 1917, and a new statue of Sakyamuni Buddha was later relocated to the temple in 1934. ==Abbots== In 1486 [[:en:Ryōan Keigo|Ryōan Keigo]] became the 171st abbot of Tōfuku-ji. At the end of the 16th century [[:en:Ankokuji Ekei|Ankokuji Ekei]] was appointed abbot. From 1980 to 2009 Tōfuku-ji has been led by head abbot [[:en:Keido Fukushima|Keidō Fukushima]].Harris, Ishwar C. ''et al.'' (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=oawiWve0GC0C ''The Laughing Buddha of Tofukuji: The Life of Zen Master Keidō Fukushima,'' p. xi.] ==Architecture== [[File:TofukujiSanmon.jpg|thumb|The main gate]] Tōfuku-ji's main gate is the oldest ''[[sanmon]]'' in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]] of Japan. It is two stories high and five bays wide. The central three bays are doors. Currently, the Tōfuku-ji complex includes 24 sub-temples, though in the past the number has been as high as 53. The complex includes Japan's oldest communal toilet, which was built in the first half of the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. In October 2022, a car crashed through and damaged the wooden doors and supporting pillars at the entrance to the toilet.{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-63304390 |publisher=[[:en:BBC News|BBC News]] |title=Japan heritage worker backs car into oldest toilet at Kyoto temple |date=18 October 2022 |access-date=18 October 2022}} ==Artwork== Tōfuku-ji's large ''nehan-zu'' painting depicts Buddha on his death bed. This massive image (7 x 14 meters) is the second largest in Japan. The image at nearby [[:en:Sennyū-ji|Sennyū-ji]] is the largest of its kind in Japan, measuring 8 x 16 meters. Both images are rarely displayed, most recently in 2003 for three days only.[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20030309k1.html "Kansai: Who -- What: Giant Buddhas shown for three days only,"] ''Japan Times Online.'' March 9, 2003. A 1238 [[:en:portrait painting|portrait painting]] of Wuzhun Shifan along with an inscription by an anonymous author was brought to the temple by Enni in the 1240s and remains there today. Plaques of Wuzhun's calligraphy are also kept at Tōfuku-ji. In 1933 the [[:en:Nihonga|Nihonga]] painter [[:en:Inshō Dōmoto|Inshō Dōmoto]] painted the large and vivid "Blue Dragon" ceiling painting for one of the halls, which he painted in 17 days.{{cite web |url=http://www.kyoto-magonote.jp/en/zen-2016/zen-2016.html |title=KYOTO MAGOnoTE｜zen-2016｜Special Spring Openings of the Zen Temples in Kyoto |website=www.kyoto-magonote.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160613164744/http://www.kyoto-magonote.jp/en/zen-2016/zen-2016.html |archive-date=2016-06-13}} ==Garden== [[File:Toufuku-ji hojyo7.JPG|thumb|right|The moss garden]] There are a number of [[:en:Japanese garden|gardens]] in the various precincts of Tōfuku-ji. The current gardens were designed by landscape architect [[:en:Mirei Shigemori|Mirei Shigemori]] in the summer and autumn of 1939. The moss garden in particular has been emblematic of the renewal of Japanese gardening principles in the 20th Century. The temple features a large number of [[:en:Acer palmatum|Japanese maple]] trees, and is most crowded during the autumn season when people flock to see the autumn foliage. It is a tradition to view the leaves from the Tsūten-kyō bridge. ==images== image:Toufuku-ji sanmon4.JPG|Main gate, side view image:FundainZunantei.jpg|View from interior image:Hiroshige People under maple trees by a stream.jpg|Scenic drawing of Tsūten-kyō bridge, by [[Hiroshige]] image:Tsuten-kyo bridge.JPG|Tsūten-kyō bridge during autumn image:Tofukuji Tsutenkyo.jpg|Tsūten-kyō image:ReiunIn GaunNoNiwa.jpg|Dry Zen garden at Reiun-in image:Toufuku-ji kaizandou3.JPG|Garden at Kaizandō image:TofukujiRyoginanToutei.jpg|Garden at Ryoginan-tōtei image:Tofukuji torii.jpg|''Torii'' gates leading to steps image:Tofukuji stairs.jpg|Steps image:Toufuku-ji hondou2.JPG|Main hall image:TofukujiKaisando.jpg|[[:en:Kaisando|Kaizandō]] image:Tofukuji-bridge-autumn-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|Maple trees in [[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] around Tsūten-kyō image:Tofukuji-maple-autumn-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of maple trees from Tsūten-kyō ==Shinan Ship== [[File:Wooden labels from Shinan Ship with Tofuku-ji written on them.jpg|thumb|Wooden Tags(木間) from the Shinan Shipwreck, ALL with Tofuku-ji(東福寺) Inscriptions]] As mentioned in the “History” section of this article, Tōfuku-ji temple was severely damaged and partly destroyed by fires during the second and third decades of the 14th century. It was specifically in response to a fire damage in 1319 that a merchant ship was sent to Yuan China in 1323 to replace damaged artifacts and to obtain special construction materials for the reconstruction. The ship that now is known as [[:en:Shinan ship|Shinan ship]], sailed from [[:en:Ningbo|Ningbo]] to Hakata. The early 14th century journey was never completed though as the ship with its 200 tons of cargo was caught in a storm and sank close to Korean shores. This became apparent only after the wreckage was found almost seven hundred years later in 1975 close to the Shinan Islands. On many of the “wooden tablets” or “wooden tags”(木間) that were used customarily to identify the cargo, the Chinese calligraphy characters of Tōfuku-ji(東福寺) temple could be clearly read. Miraculously her passengers, some (or many) of them Japanese Buddhist monks, as recorded by the Korean annals [[:en:Goryeosa|Goryeosa]], drifted ashore sound and safe. We can also find records of this unfavorable but overall lucky incident in the personal notes of zen monk-poet [[:en:Daichi Sokei|Daichi Zenji]](大智禅師) who happened to be one of the repatriated passengers.{{Cite web|date=2019-07-05|title=The Return of the Celadons|url=https://gwangjunewsgic.com/arts-culture/exhibitions/the-return-of-the-celadons/|access-date=2024-03-09|website=[[Gwangju News]]}}{{Cite web|date=2021-04-24|title=新案船 (Shinan Ship) Folder|url=https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=Iw87eZhyO0A&t=2s&pp=ygURU2hpbmFuIHNoaXAgZm9sZGU%3D|access-date=2024-03-09|website= titled video, please check at 2 min 02 sec. to see “Tofuku-ji” characters on one of the wooden labels(from [[youtube]] channel of user “chang gyong”)}} ==See also== *[[:en:Zen|Zen]] *[[:en:Enni|Enni]] *[[:en:Wuzhun Shifan|Wuzhun Shifan]] *[[:en:List of Buddhist temples|List of Buddhist temples]] *[[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC ''The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism.''] New York: Rosen Publishing Group. {{ISBN|978-0-8239-2240-6}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/42680558 OCLC 42680558] * [[:en:Heinrich Dumoulin|Dumoulin]], Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: [[:en:World Wisdom|World Wisdom]]. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * [[:en:Keido Fukushima|Fukushima]], Keidō and Fumi Dan. (2006). ''Tōfukuji.'' Kyoto: Tankōsha. {{ISBN|978-4-473-03353-6}}; {{OCLC|145752382}} * Harris, Ishwar C. and Jeff Shore. (2004). [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/56051074 ''The Laughing Buddha of Tofukuji: The Life of Zen Master Keidō Fukushima.''] Bloomington, Indiana: World Wisdom. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-62-4}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/56051074 OCLC 56051074] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |type= Hardback |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * [http://www.tofukuji.jp Tōfuku-ji official web site] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/tofukuji/ Tōfuku-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/05tofuku.html Tōfuku-ji] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?id=120,318,0,0,1,0 Tōfuku-ji] * [http://damien.douxchamps.net/photo/japan/kansai/kyoto/tofukuji Pictures of Tōfuku-ji] {{Commons Category|Tofukuji|Tōfuku-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1230s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1236 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Rinzai temples]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Zen gardens]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] }} [[en:Tōfuku-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ar:توفوكو-جي]] [[arz:توفوكو جى]] [[ca:Tōfuku-ji]] {{Commons category|Tofukuji}} [[de:Tōfuku-ji]] [[el:Τοφούκου-τζι]] [[fa:توفوکو-جی]] [[fr:Tōfuku-ji]] [[gan:東福寺]] [[id:Tōfuku-ji]] [[it:Tōfuku-ji]] [[ja:東福寺]] [[ko:도후쿠지]] [[pl:Tōfuku-ji]] [[pnb:ٹوفوکو-جی]] [[ru:Тофуку-дзи]] [[tr:Tōfuku-ji]] [[uk:Тофуку-дзі]] [[wuu:东福寺]] [[zh:東福寺]] [[zh-classical:東福寺]] {{wikidata link|Q2166743}}

In 1881, a fire burned down many major buildings such as the Main Hall, the ''Hōjo'', the ''Hattō'' and the statue of Sakyamuni Buddha. During the [[:en:Russo-Japanese War|Russo-Japanese War]], the temple area was requisitioned and became a [[:en:prisoner-of-war camp|prisoner-of-war camp]] for Russians. Both the main hall and the ''Hattō'' were rebuilt in 1917, and a new statue of Sakyamuni Buddha was later relocated to the temple in 1934. ==Abbots== In 1486 [[:en:Ryōan Keigo|Ryōan Keigo]] became the 171st abbot of Tōfuku-ji. At the end of the 16th century [[:en:Ankokuji Ekei|Ankokuji Ekei]] was appointed abbot. From 1980 to 2009 Tōfuku-ji has been led by head abbot [[:en:Keido Fukushima|Keidō Fukushima]].Harris, Ishwar C. ''et al.'' (2004). [https://books.google.com/books?id=oawiWve0GC0C ''The Laughing Buddha of Tofukuji: The Life of Zen Master Keidō Fukushima,'' p. xi.] ==Architecture== [[File:TofukujiSanmon.jpg|thumb|The main gate]] Tōfuku-ji's main gate is the oldest ''[[sanmon]]'' in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is a [[:en:National Treasure (Japan)|National Treasure]] of Japan. It is two stories high and five bays wide. The central three bays are doors. Currently, the Tōfuku-ji complex includes 24 sub-temples, though in the past the number has been as high as 53. The complex includes Japan's oldest communal toilet, which was built in the first half of the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]. In October 2022, a car crashed through and damaged the wooden doors and supporting pillars at the entrance to the toilet.{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-63304390 |publisher=[[:en:BBC News|BBC News]] |title=Japan heritage worker backs car into oldest toilet at Kyoto temple |date=18 October 2022 |access-date=18 October 2022}} ==Artwork== Tōfuku-ji's large ''nehan-zu'' painting depicts Buddha on his death bed. This massive image (7 x 14 meters) is the second largest in Japan. The image at nearby [[:en:Sennyū-ji|Sennyū-ji]] is the largest of its kind in Japan, measuring 8 x 16 meters. Both images are rarely displayed, most recently in 2003 for three days only.[http://search.japantimes.co.jp/cgi-bin/nn20030309k1.html "Kansai: Who -- What: Giant Buddhas shown for three days only,"] ''Japan Times Online.'' March 9, 2003. A 1238 [[:en:portrait painting|portrait painting]] of Wuzhun Shifan along with an inscription by an anonymous author was brought to the temple by Enni in the 1240s and remains there today. Plaques of Wuzhun's calligraphy are also kept at Tōfuku-ji. In 1933 the [[:en:Nihonga|Nihonga]] painter [[:en:Inshō Dōmoto|Inshō Dōmoto]] painted the large and vivid "Blue Dragon" ceiling painting for one of the halls, which he painted in 17 days.{{cite web |url=http://www.kyoto-magonote.jp/en/zen-2016/zen-2016.html |title=KYOTO MAGOnoTE｜zen-2016｜Special Spring Openings of the Zen Temples in Kyoto |website=www.kyoto-magonote.jp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160613164744/http://www.kyoto-magonote.jp/en/zen-2016/zen-2016.html |archive-date=2016-06-13}} ==Garden== [[File:Toufuku-ji hojyo7.JPG|thumb|right|The moss garden]] There are a number of [[:en:Japanese garden|gardens]] in the various precincts of Tōfuku-ji. The current gardens were designed by landscape architect [[:en:Mirei Shigemori|Mirei Shigemori]] in the summer and autumn of 1939. The moss garden in particular has been emblematic of the renewal of Japanese gardening principles in the 20th Century. The temple features a large number of [[:en:Acer palmatum|Japanese maple]] trees, and is most crowded during the autumn season when people flock to see the autumn foliage. It is a tradition to view the leaves from the Tsūten-kyō bridge. ==images== image:Toufuku-ji sanmon4.JPG|Main gate, side view image:FundainZunantei.jpg|View from interior image:Hiroshige People under maple trees by a stream.jpg|Scenic drawing of Tsūten-kyō bridge, by [[Hiroshige]] image:Tsuten-kyo bridge.JPG|Tsūten-kyō bridge during autumn image:Tofukuji Tsutenkyo.jpg|Tsūten-kyō image:ReiunIn GaunNoNiwa.jpg|Dry Zen garden at Reiun-in image:Toufuku-ji kaizandou3.JPG|Garden at Kaizandō image:TofukujiRyoginanToutei.jpg|Garden at Ryoginan-tōtei image:Tofukuji torii.jpg|''Torii'' gates leading to steps image:Tofukuji stairs.jpg|Steps image:Toufuku-ji hondou2.JPG|Main hall image:TofukujiKaisando.jpg|[[:en:Kaisando|Kaizandō]] image:Tofukuji-bridge-autumn-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|Maple trees in [[:en:Autumn foliage|Autumn foliage]] around Tsūten-kyō image:Tofukuji-maple-autumn-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|View of maple trees from Tsūten-kyō ==Shinan Ship== [[File:Wooden labels from Shinan Ship with Tofuku-ji written on them.jpg|thumb|Wooden Tags(木間) from the Shinan Shipwreck, ALL with Tofuku-ji(東福寺) Inscriptions]] As mentioned in the “History” section of this article, Tōfuku-ji temple was severely damaged and partly destroyed by fires during the second and third decades of the 14th century. It was specifically in response to a fire damage in 1319 that a merchant ship was sent to Yuan China in 1323 to replace damaged artifacts and to obtain special construction materials for the reconstruction. The ship that now is known as [[:en:Shinan ship|Shinan ship]], sailed from [[:en:Ningbo|Ningbo]] to Hakata. The early 14th century journey was never completed though as the ship with its 200 tons of cargo was caught in a storm and sank close to Korean shores. This became apparent only after the wreckage was found almost seven hundred years later in 1975 close to the Shinan Islands. On many of the “wooden tablets” or “wooden tags”(木間) that were used customarily to identify the cargo, the Chinese calligraphy characters of Tōfuku-ji(東福寺) temple could be clearly read. Miraculously her passengers, some (or many) of them Japanese Buddhist monks, as recorded by the Korean annals [[:en:Goryeosa|Goryeosa]], drifted ashore sound and safe. We can also find records of this unfavorable but overall lucky incident in the personal notes of zen monk-poet [[:en:Daichi Sokei|Daichi Zenji]](大智禅師) who happened to be one of the repatriated passengers.{{Cite web|date=2019-07-05|title=The Return of the Celadons|url=https://gwangjunewsgic.com/arts-culture/exhibitions/the-return-of-the-celadons/|access-date=2024-03-09|website=[[Gwangju News]]}}{{Cite web|date=2021-04-24|title=新案船 (Shinan Ship) Folder|url=https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=Iw87eZhyO0A&t=2s&pp=ygURU2hpbmFuIHNoaXAgZm9sZGU%3D|access-date=2024-03-09|website= titled video, please check at 2 min 02 sec. to see “Tofuku-ji” characters on one of the wooden labels(from [[youtube]] channel of user “chang gyong”)}} ==See also== *[[:en:Zen|Zen]] *[[:en:Enni|Enni]] *[[:en:Wuzhun Shifan|Wuzhun Shifan]] *[[:en:List of Buddhist temples|List of Buddhist temples]] *[[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (paintings)]] *[[:en:en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (writings)]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} == References == * Baroni, Helen Josephine. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC ''The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism.''] New York: Rosen Publishing Group. {{ISBN|978-0-8239-2240-6}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/42680558 OCLC 42680558] * [[:en:Heinrich Dumoulin|Dumoulin]], Heinrich. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC ''Zen Buddhism: A History'' (Vol. II: Japan).] Bloomington, Indiana: [[:en:World Wisdom|World Wisdom]]. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-90-7}} * [[:en:Keido Fukushima|Fukushima]], Keidō and Fumi Dan. (2006). ''Tōfukuji.'' Kyoto: Tankōsha. {{ISBN|978-4-473-03353-6}}; {{OCLC|145752382}} * Harris, Ishwar C. and Jeff Shore. (2004). [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/56051074 ''The Laughing Buddha of Tofukuji: The Life of Zen Master Keidō Fukushima.''] Bloomington, Indiana: World Wisdom. {{ISBN|978-0-941532-62-4}}; [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/56051074 OCLC 56051074] * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1956). ''Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869.'' Kyoto: The Ponsonby Memorial Society. ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |type= Hardback |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * [http://www.tofukuji.jp Tōfuku-ji official web site] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/tofukuji/ Tōfuku-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/05tofuku.html Tōfuku-ji] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?id=120,318,0,0,1,0 Tōfuku-ji] * [http://damien.douxchamps.net/photo/japan/kansai/kyoto/tofukuji Pictures of Tōfuku-ji] {{Commons Category|Tofukuji|Tōfuku-ji}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:1230s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:1236 establishments in Asia]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Rinzai temples]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:13th-century Buddhist temples]] [[Category:Zen gardens]] [[Category:Temples of Gautama Buddha]] }} [[en:Tōfuku-ji]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ar:توفوكو-جي]] [[arz:توفوكو جى]] [[ca:Tōfuku-ji]] {{Commons category|Tofukuji}} [[de:Tōfuku-ji]] [[el:Τοφούκου-τζι]] [[fa:توفوکو-جی]] [[fr:Tōfuku-ji]] [[gan:東福寺]] [[id:Tōfuku-ji]] [[it:Tōfuku-ji]] [[ja:東福寺]] [[ko:도후쿠지]] [[pl:Tōfuku-ji]] [[pnb:ٹوفوکو-جی]] [[ru:Тофуку-дзи]] [[tr:Tōfuku-ji]] [[uk:Тофуку-дзі]] [[wuu:东福寺]] [[zh:東福寺]] [[zh-classical:東福寺]] {{wikidata link|Q2166743}}
